blocks.content: Cassandra was in bed when the message came. Not asleep — she'd been lying in the dark with the day spread across her, the way good days sometimes needed to be absorbed without light.
Her phone lit up on the nightstand. It was Maya.
Cass. Are you awake?
She looked at the message for a moment before answering.
Yes.
Can we talk? Not a call. Just — this is okay.
Okay.
A long pause. Long enough that Cassandra could picture Maya on the other end, typing and deleting the way she did when a thing was hard to get right.
I talked to Dad tonight. About you. About things.
I know. He said you might reach out.
He told you that?
He said sometimes you need to talk through the door before you can open it.
Another pause.

I don't know how to say this right. I've been trying to find the right way to say it for weeks and I think the problem is there isn't one. So I'm just going to say it even though it's going to come out messy.
Say it messy. I'll understand.

I'm scared. That's the whole of it. I'm scared all the time. And when I see you happy — really happy, not the kind you perform — I get more scared, not less. Because happy means invested. Invested means there's more to lose. And I can't—
I know.

I don't know how to watch you build something knowing what I know. I don't know how to be supportive of something that's going to cost you. I know that's not fair. I know you didn't ask me to protect you from your own life. I just — it's not a switch I can turn off, Cass.
I'm not asking you to turn it off. I'm asking you to carry it somewhere I can't see it all the time.
A longer pause.
He seems like a good person. From what I've heard.
He is.
Does he know? About any of it?
Not yet.
Are you going to tell him?
Yes. When it's the right time.
How do you know when that is?
The same way you know when a piece of writing is finished. Not because there's nothing more you could say — but because the next thing you'd add would start taking something away. I'll know.
A very long pause. The kind that meant Maya was sitting somewhere with the phone in her hands, deciding.
I don't like this. I want to. But I don't. Not yet. Maybe not for a while.
I know.
But I see you. And I'm not going to stand in the way of it.
Cassandra read that last message three times.
Thank you. That's everything. That's exactly everything.
Just be careful with yourself. Not careful like — don't live carefully. Just careful with yourself. You matter past the story. Past what happens. Okay?
Okay.
Goodnight, Cass.
Goodnight, Maya.

She set the phone face-down on the nightstand. Outside, the town had gone quiet. She looked at her hand — the faint blue paint still there between her fingers if the light was right, proof of an afternoon.
She looked at the ceiling the way she sometimes did when she was landing somewhere.
She thought about Ethan's apartment. His sketchbook. The way he had said take all the time you need, like time was something he had plenty of, and would share.
Okay.
She closed her eyes.
